draft.description:
The author is writing or revising the document. Free editing.
May be re-entered from in_review when the reviewer asks for
revisions, keeping the same document identifier.

withdraw.description:
Withdraw is only legal from draft. After assignment, the author
cannot quietly retract; the reviewer must close the document
via reject or request_revisions followed by archive.

reviewer_assigned.description:
A reviewer has been assigned; the document is in their queue but
they have not started reviewing yet.

in_review.description:
The reviewer has the document open and is forming a decision:
approve, reject, or send back for revisions.

approved.description:
The reviewer approved the document. Publication is now permitted
but not automatic. The document remains unpublished until an
explicit publish action runs.

archive.description:
An approved document that is never published may be archived.
Closes the lifecycle without release; preserves the approval as
historical record.

rejected.description:
The reviewer rejected the document. The document can only be
archived from here; reopening for a new attempt requires creating
a fresh document with a new identifier.

published.description:
The document has been released to its intended audience. From
published the only legitimate next step in this model is
archiving.

archive.description:
A published document is archived when superseded or retired.
The model does not track the superseding document; that is a
separate concern (see open_points on versioning).

archived.description:
Terminal: the document is stored for the record and is no longer
part of the active set. Archive may be reached from approved
(never published in time), published (superseded), or rejected.

withdrawn.description:
Terminal: the author retracted the document before review
started. Preserves the document as "considered, not pursued".

pom.description:
Lifecycle of an internal document that goes through review, approval,
and publication. A document starts as draft, is assigned to a
reviewer, may be approved or rejected (or sent back for revisions),
may be published after approval, and is eventually archived. The
author may withdraw the document only before review starts.

events.name: assign_reviewer
events.description:
An editor or the author assigns a reviewer. Triggered manually,
not derived from state.

events.name: start_review
events.description: The assigned reviewer opens the document and starts evaluating.

events.name: approve
events.description:
The reviewer approves the document. Conditional on the actor
having the approver role.

events.name: reject
events.description:
The reviewer rejects the document. Conditional on the actor
having the approver role.

events.name: request_revisions
events.description:
The reviewer asks the author to revise. The document returns to
draft so the author can edit freely. Conditional on the actor
having the approver role.

events.name: publish
events.description:
The approved document is released. Distinct from approve to
preserve the option of staged release, scheduled publication,
and a final pre-publication checklist.

events.name: archive
events.description:
The document is moved to the archive. Reachable from approved,
published, or rejected — three distinct semantic motivations,
same destination.

events.name: withdraw
events.description: The author retracts the document. Only legal from draft.

guards.name: is_approver
guards.description:
The actor performing the transition holds the approver role
defined by the target project. The schema does not model roles
as first-class entities in v1; the role check lives in target
code, behind this named predicate. See open_points.

guards.name: has_required_signoffs
guards.description:
The target project's publication policy is satisfied: legal,
branding, accessibility, or whatever the policy requires. The
schema does not enumerate the signoffs; the predicate is opaque
and lives in target code.

invariants:
Approval is not publication. A document in state approved is not
yet visible to the intended audience. The two states are
deliberately separate so that staged release, scheduled
publication, and a final pre-publication checklist remain
possible without changing the model.

Withdrawal is only legal from draft. After reviewer_assigned the
document must reach a terminal state through the review flow:
reject + archive, or approve + (publish | archive). This mirrors
spec-evolution's "no quiet retraction after review starts".

Archive has three distinct semantic sources: approved (never
published), published (retired or superseded), rejected (closed
as not pursued). The model keeps them as separate transitions
so the history records which path was taken.

Rejected is not terminal. It admits one transition: archive. The
intent is to make archival explicit even for rejections, so
every document ends in archived or withdrawn — never lingering
in rejected.
open_points:
Parallel / multi-signoff approval. Real organizations often
require multiple approvers in parallel or in sequence (legal,
branding, technical). v1 of the schema models single sequential
approval. If usage demands it, future schema may need a
"parallel_regions" concept (heavy) or a convention for chaining
multiple in_review-equivalent states (lighter). Declared out of
scope for the experiment.
Role as schema entity. is_approver and has_required_signoffs are
named guards whose role semantics live in the target code's
authorization layer. The schema does not declare roles as
first-class. Pro: keeps the schema minimal and target-agnostic.
Con: the model does not visually expose "who can do what"; that
lives in the implementation guide and target code. Worth
reviewing when more workflows accumulate role-based gating.
Versioning of published documents. published -> archived via
archive is fine when retiring; supersession by a newer version
is not modeled here. Two options for future schema: explicit
supersede event (mirroring spec-evolution), or external
relation linking the archive event to the superseding document
identifier. Out of scope for v1.
rejected as non-terminal but with only one outgoing transition
(archive). Borderline case: should the schema let archive be
automatic from rejected after N days? That would require timer
semantics, already declared out of scope. For now, archive from
rejected is an explicit action.
closed_points:
"How to model multiple terminal flavors with distinct
semantics?" — same pattern used in ticket-lifecycle (closed,
duplicate, wont_fix). Here: archived and withdrawn. The schema
handles it cleanly via multiple is_final states reached by
different transitions.
